entry.title: Embrace the Change: Champion the Composable Enterprise
entry.content:
Unsure about your existing systems and lacking visibility into your business processes?

Don't know which solution to choose?

Overwhelmed by countless pages of system setup?

Facing lengthy implementation projects that drag on for months?

In today's rapidly evolving world, agility is essential for business success. However, the fear of disrupting existing systems and the challenges of adopting new solutions can hold you back. So, what can you do?

When you consider it, what companies truly need are business processes or capabilities. They aren't concerned with the underlying systems or how to connect them. Imagine if you could purchase these readily available capabilities on the market, plug them in, and compose your own processes. After purchasing, a simple one-click installation would have your processes up and running in minutes instead of months, all while keeping technical complexities hidden. If a particular capability doesn't meet your needs, you can easily remove it. Plus, you'll only pay for what you use through a subscription-based model. Additionally, you'll gain greater clarity on your processes and discover new ideas for improvement.

Does this sound like a dream? This is the essence of a Composable Enterprise. If you're eager to learn more and turn this vision into reality, join us on this exciting journey with SAP enterprise architects as we explore this strategic topic and work towards making future enterprises more composable. Sign up using the link below: 

Composable Enterprise: Reimagining Enterprise Software for Unprecedented Business Agility 

Customer registration for the project is open until November 14. Once registered, you'll receive an invitation to an introductory call.

entry.title: How SAP Business AI and Large Language Models are shaping the future of SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP
entry.content:
Terry Penner and Jürgen Butsmann discuss the latest developments in SAP Business AI for SAP S/4HANA and SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).  The conversation focuses on how Artificial Intelligence is evolving at SAP, with particular attention to large language models (LLMs) and their growing application in SAP cloud ERP and SAP BTP.

Listen to the podcast: Spotify | Apple Podcasts

Topics discussed:

Part 1: Evolution of AI and Large Language Models (LLMs)

  • Latest developments in the AI market
  • SAP’s ERP strategy evolution for AI
  • Latest solution architecture updates

Part 2:  Differentiating the AI solutions in SAP cloud ERP

  • What is Joule
  • Differentiating AI offerings
  • How can AI add value to a business
  • Partner role with SAP Business AI

Part 3: Cloud ERP AI Priorities

  • SAP cloud ERP AI development priorities
  • SAP’s Business AI scenario priorities

This blog is a follow-up to AI for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP: deep-dive discussion

About the speakers:

Jürgen Butsmann is part of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Product Marketing team responsible for AI in the context of SAP S/4HANA, finance and digital supply chain. Jürgen has been with SAP for more than 26 years.  He is a recognized expert for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP .

Terry Penner is part of the SAP BTP marketing team for Business Applications, focused on SAP BTP for SAP S/4HANA. He has more than 23 years of technical and business experience in the SAP platform and analytics, including working directly on hundreds of customer implementations.

Part 1: Evolution of AI and Large Language Models (LLMs)

Latest developments in the AI market

Terry: In today's discussion, we'll explore how Artificial Intelligence for SAP S/4HANA and the SAP Business Technology Platform is evolving. Jürgen, what new developments have occurred in the AI market since we last spoke?

Jürgen: The evolution of technology continues unabated. We're observing an increase in services such as document services and advanced large language models (LLMs). The LLMs we see today are notably more fact-based, not just designed for entertainment purposes like the early iterations of ChatGPT, but for fact-oriented applications like those seen with Aleph Alpha. Additionally, there have been multiple iterations and diverse models emerging tailored to specific needs. We've also noticed a decline in pricing, which makes these AI services increasingly attractive and accessible in the market.

Terry: Absolutely. We've also witnessed significant legal developments, with content owners like The New York Times and others looking to withdraw their material from large language models, underscoring the importance of having robust and compliant data sources for these models.

SAP’s ERP strategy evolution for AI

Terry: How has SAP's ERP strategy for AI evolved?

Jürgen: Our strategy has evolved significantly, placing a more substantial emphasis on an AI-first approach across SAP. This shift means a lot of new ideas and pre-made implementations for our customers. We've taken a two-pronged approach in our investments. First, we're enhancing end-user productivity by integrating digital assistants into our systems, with Joule being a primary component. Although still early in deployment, these tools signify a shift from traditional graphical user interfaces to more intuitive digital assistant interactions.

Further confirming this direction, our CEO, Christian Klein, anticipates that in ten years, most system users will interact with digital assistants rather than traditional interfaces. Our focus also extends to natural language interactions within these systems, fostering intelligent activities and responses.

 

Secondly, we are concentrating on enhancing end-to-end processes. While we continue to enhance user interaction and productivity on the front end, significant efforts are directed towards optimizing large-scale processes, particularly within enterprise service management. This holistic approach ensures not just improvements in individual functionalities but also the acceleration of end-to-end process flows, all underpinned by advanced AI and generative AI technologies. This dual focus aligns with our commitment to transforming business processes through innovative AI applications.

Latest solution architecture updates

Terry: What are the latest updates to SAP's AI solution architecture since then?

Jürgen: One major change is the ongoing development of Joule. Initially, Joule had a rather rigid architecture that was not very flexible and did not support LLMs. This has changed. We have introduced the capability to create prompts using LLM support, which is a priority that has helped increase acceptance of this digital system and co-pilot for everyday work. Additionally, we've improved context sensitivity—knowing where you are in your system and what steps you are processing. This is a key differentiator from other co-pilots on the market and allows us to integrate responses that feel less artificial and more contextually aware.  It's able to  integrate proprietary documents into the context of the business processes.

Furthermore, we continue to develop its extensibility. We've opened possibilities for customers to tailor the co-pilot to perform functions specific to their needs.

Terry: And regarding extensibility, that’s a significant area where SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) collaborates with the ERP team by supplying developer tools and experiences, such as the vector engine within SAP HANA, to support these functionalities—it's a joint effort.

Part 2:  Differentiating the AI solutions in SAP cloud ERP

What is Joule?

Terry: We’ve spoken quite a bit about Joule. Could you clarify exactly what Joule is?

Jürgen: Essentially, Joule serves as an interface for natural language commands within our system architecture. It acts as an entry point where users can interact with the system to trigger functions or retrieve necessary business process information. While Joule leverages AI technologies, its use doesn’t alter the inherent capabilities of our AI. It fits seamlessly into our system, making those use cases distinct and noteworthy. Ideally, Joule simplifies user interactions by providing a natural, conversational interface with the system. While our systems could operate without Joule, using it enhances the user experience, making interactions more engaging and straightforward.

  

Differentiating the AI solutions in SAP cloud ERP

Terry: What's the difference with SAP Business AI? How should customers differentiate potential AI offerings?

Jürgen: The top priority for SAP Business AI is to provide clear and measurable value to customers. Before using AI scenarios, there must be a reasonable and measurable return on investment. The focus is on implementing AI in SAP systems without the need to change generic functionality, where the system's context details can be used to address processes more effectively.

The reliability of AI use cases is a major focus, based on fact-based foundation models whenever possible. All AI-assisted processes undergo an ethical check and are supported by the data security of SAP Business AI architecture.

We are also continuing to refine our pricing approach and evaluating if we will continue using current AI units or shift to different technologies in the future. Customers consider usage, potential value add, costs, and investments when determining the break-even point for their use cases. Moreover, customers expect our AI solutions to be user-friendly.

Terry: Adding to that, not all AI use cases deliver sufficient value for application. It's crucial to concentrate on scenarios with high-quality underlying data linked to large language models. For example, one effective use of AI I've observed involves searching through support cases and providing recommendations to new team members, enhancing the search experience and improving how information is interpreted and suggested.

How can AI add value to a business

Terry: How would you explain to a customer how AI can add value to a business?

Jürgen: When considering how AI supports business processes, the user's connection to the system is paramount. Ideally, this happens through a digital co-pilot, like Joule in our case. Users interact with Joule by posing questions that prompt queries to a large language model, which in turn provides responses. However, large language models may lack specific business process details since those are unique and not universally predefined, leading to potentially incomplete answers. 

 

To address this, we might extend our architecture to incorporate calls to the ERP system or access other proprietary data, ensuring no sensitive business information is exposed to potentially vulnerable cloud environments. We balance accessibility with security by using a vector database to locate and integrate the right business-specific data from outside the large language model's scope.

As users submit human language requests, these are processed through the digital system and converted into technical prompts by a large language model. These prompts trigger the vector engine to retrieve the necessary information. The returning data can then be refined further by the large language models, which are all deployed securely within our system environment to prevent data leakage.

The data retrieval process benefits from enhancements such as semantic similarities and pattern recognition to continuously improve response accuracy. Once processed, the information is delivered back to the user, completing the cycle and delivering the value we aim for with our AI-enabled systems.

This systematic approach ensures that AI not only supports but also enhances business processes effectively while maintaining data integrity and security.

Terry: So, we could describe Joule as the front-end experience, distinct from the backend LLM that powers the AI functionalities.

Partner role with SAP Business AI

Terry: What role do partners play in this space?

Jürgen: Firstly, our partners have a deep understanding of our customers. It's crucial that partners are well-versed in our setup, strategy, architecture, pricing, and other elements that shape how we envision utilizing these services. We leverage our partners to create scalable solutions that precisely address specific customer needs. They are instrumental in implementing individual projects that tackle unique business challenges at the customer sites. Our partners have a proven track record of handling such use cases effectively.

Part 3: AI Extensibility and SAP BTP

SAP cloud ERP AI development priorities

Terry: What are some of the other AI development priorities at SAP cloud ERP, and how is SAP BTP supporting those AI scenarios?

 

Jürgen: We're integrating various technologies beyond what we've already discussed, like our work with live language models. For example, merging our capabilities with Microsoft Copilot offers expanded possibilities for enhancing natural language interactions. SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and the AI Foundation are pivotal in anchoring these flexibilities. This integration allows us to broaden our scope and reinforce the support we provide for cloud ERP AI scenarios.

SAP’s Business AI scenario priorities

Terry: What business scenarios are central to SAP's Business AI focus?

Jürgen: Our focus isn't narrow, but we do see significant interest particularly in the finance sector. Currently, we're engaging extensively with businesses, customers, and partners to pinpoint key priorities specific to finance. Our approach begins by assessing what immediate actions we can take and then contemplating more visionary possibilities for the future. For example, one idea is the automatic creation of forecast scenarios based on historical data within our systems. This capability could enhance management, strategy, and decision-making meetings significantly, even without all finance experts gathered at one table. Additionally, there are numerous other aspects of finance where AI can play a transformative role.

Terry: We will link to some specific use cases within the podcast notes. Thank you, Jürgen, for joining us and sharing your insights into AI for cloud ERP and the business technology platform. It's been enlightening.

Jürgen: Thank you for having me. I hope our discussion helps people understand better where we are now and where we are headed with our technology.

[ Note:  This blog was adapted from the podcast transcript by an SAP system using Azure OpenAI–gpt-4 ]

Key links:

  • Four ways to infuse business-ready, trusted AI into your processes
  • SAP BTP for S/4HANA
  • SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Business AI

Read more in this series:

  • Artificial Intelligence (AI) for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP: A deep dive
  • SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and Master Data Management Best Practices with SAP BTP
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Extensions with SAP Build Best Practices: An Expert Roundtable
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Extensions and Clean Core with SAP BTP - A Best Practices Conversation

entry.title: A Leap Forward in Encryption: TLS 1.3 Support in S/4HANA
entry.content:
TLS is a cryptographic protocol designed to provide secure communication over a computer network. It is used to establish a secure connection between a client and a server to ensure that the data exchanged is private and reliable.

The Benefits of TLS 1.3

The introduction of TLS1.3 in SAP S/4HANA 2023 brings several benefits designed to enhance security and streamline processes:

  1. Simplified and stronger cipher suites: TLS 1.3 removes support for outdated and potentially insecure algorithms and ciphers, focusing on a more streamlined and secure set. This simplification reduces the potential for misconfigurations and vulnerabilities while strengthening the overall security posture of your communications.
  2. Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) always enabled: With PFS enabled, each TLS session has a unique session key which cannot be recovered by learning the private key, ensuring that your session keys will not be compromised even if the server's private key is. In TLS1.3, PFS is always enabled, enhancing the confidentiality of your data.
  3. Simplified key exchange: TLS 1.3 only supports Diffie-Hellman type key exchange algorithms, removing support for RSA key exchange. This simplifies the key exchange process and eliminates potential vulnerabilities associated with RSA key exchange.

How to Configure TLS 1.3 in SAP S/4HANA OnPremise

Before configuring TLS 1.3, please make sure your system fits the version and patch requirements as detailed in SAP Note 3318423.

TLS versions can be configured via profile parameters. The following cipher suites are recommended by SAP for maximum interoperability, activating TLS 1.2 - 1.3 on the server (inbound) side, and TLS 1.0 - 1.3 on the client (outbound) side:

 Parameter NameParameter Value
Server (inbound)ssl/ciphersuites1569:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH
Client (outbound)ssl/client_ciphersuites1174:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH

As a note of caution: If your previous inbound configuration included TLS 1.0 - 1.1, please take a look at SAP Note 3346659 for how to safely restart your system after deactivating TLS 1.0 - 1.1.

If you prefer to have your inbound side open to TLS 1.0 - 1.1 as well as TLS 1.2 - 1.3, you can use the following parameter instead of the above one:

 Parameter NameParameter Value
Server (inbound)ssl/ciphersuites1159:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH

If you would like to restrict your outbound communication to TLS 1.2 and 1.3 only, the following values are recommended:

 Parameter NameParameter Value
Client (outbound)ssl/client_ciphersuites1584:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH

Please note that SAP currently does not recommend setting the cipher suite string parameter to TLS 1.2 and 1.3 for the outbound side, as this may cause interoperability issues when trying to communicate with older servers. Especially older embedded devices often do not support TLS 1.2 and upwards. Therefore, please verify that you have no incompatible servers in your system landscape before making such a change. You can read more about how to safely configure restrictive parameters for your system in SAP Note 3346659.

It is also possible to configure TLS 1.3 for specific clients in transaction sm59. For type G or H destinations, go to the tab "Logon & Security" and add your custom cipher suite string value in the field "TLS Cipher Suites".

This approach is especially recommended if you would like to activate TLS 1.3 systemwide, but have specific servers in your landscape which do not support TLS 1.3 and behave erroneously when a client tries to establish a TLS 1.3 connection. In this case, you can disable TLS 1.3 for these specific servers via the configuration shown in the above image.

Please also see the sm59 documentation for more details.

TLS 1.3 in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

TLS 1.3 is activated by default in all SAP S/4HANA Cloud Web Dispatchers. You can read more about it in this article.

Relevant SAP Notes for Further Information

SAP has released several SAP Notes explaining which software versions, patch levels, and configurations are required to enable TLS 1.3 for SAP S/4HANA:

3318423 - Is TLS 1.3 Supported by SAP Kernel for ABAP and SAP S/4HANA?
3346659 - TLS Ciphersuite String Configuration for ABAP and SAP Kernel-Based Systems

Conclusion

The support of TLS 1.3 is a significant leap forward for SAP S/4HANA 2023. It simplifies configurations, enhances security through stronger cipher suites, and ensures the safety of your communication through PFS and a simplified key exchange.

entry.title: Implement Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Objective

Currency Conversion is an important aspect of any data modelling, planning, and reporting. SAP Datasphere software provides a standard functionality for Currency conversion. However, this is a quick step by step process on how currency conversion can be implemented with federation using S/4 HANA as a source. This article is completely based on my learning experience. I hope, this article will be useful for those consultants who are looking to capitalize the virtualization, federation, and tailor the needs based of the custom requirement.

How is it  different from standard approach?

1. This approach does not need all the currency relevant tables for the basic currency conversion but have the minimum four tables that are primarily involved as mentioned in the pre-requisites

2. Tables are federated or remote or virtual physical present in S/4 HANA. Unless there is a need for persistency can be scheduled at the input layer

3. Tables when remotely configured can be named according the naming conventions of the business need purposes

Prerequisites

  • Connection of SAP S/4 HANA on Premise or Cloud systems
  • Below standard tables activated in S/4 HANA systems

Table Name

Table Description

TCURV

 Exchange rate types

TCURX

 Decimal places in currencies

TCURR

 Exchange rates

TCURF

 Conversion factors

Option 1:

 Code snippet for SQL view

SELECT "FISCALYEARPERIOD", "PRODUCT","COMPANYCODE", "TRANSACTIONCURRENCY","AMOUNTINTRANSACTIONCURRENCY"

 

CONVERT_CURRENCY( AMOUNT => "AMOUNTINTRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

SOURCE_UNIT => "TRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

   "CONVERSION_TYPE" => 'M',

    "REFERENCE_DATE" => '2024-09-01', ----can be an input parameter

    "CLIENT" => '400',

    "SCHEMA" => 'DW_DEV_SCHEMA',

    "ERROR_HANDLING" => 'set_to_null',

    "TARGET_UNIT" => 'SAR', -----can be an input parameter

"PRECISIONS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURX', --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

    "CONFIGURATION_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURV', --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

    "PREFACTORS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURF', --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

    "RATES_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURR' --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

/***These remote tables when imported have been named with prefix as S4H in this case.

)

AS deb_cre_lc,

 

from "FIGL_LINEITEM" ----ACDOCA standard SAP Transaction Table

Resultset:

When converted from USD to SAR

Option 2:

Case of calculated column in a graphical view

Calculated column dbe_cre_lc definition

Code snippet

CONVERT_CURRENCY( AMOUNT => "AMOUNTINTRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

SOURCE_UNIT => "TRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

   "CONVERSION_TYPE" => 'M',

    "REFERENCE_DATE" => :IP_REFERENCE_DATE,

    "CLIENT" => '400',

    "SCHEMA" => ‘DW_DEV_SCHEMA',

    "ERROR_HANDLING" => 'set_to_null',

    "TARGET_UNIT" => :IP_TARGET_CURRENCY,

"PRECISIONS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURX',---federated tables from S/4 HANA

"CONFIGURATION_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURV', ,---federated tables from S/4 HANA

"PREFACTORS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURF', ,---federated tables from S/4 HANA

"RATES_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURR') ,---federated tables from S/4 HANA

INPUT PARAMETERS

Resultset:

entry.title: New Lead to Cash Variant: Lead to Cash (Consumer Industries B2C) for Cloud Deployment
entry.content:
With Lead to Cash (Consumer Industries B2C) for Cloud Deployment, we  are introducing a new reference Business Process Variant of SAP Lead to Cash.

It is a template process based on best practices as well as common scenarios, and can be adapted or extended based on your specific requirements.

In today's competitive consumer industries landscape, businesses are constantly seeking innovative solutions to streamline operations, enhance customer experiences, and drive sales performance. The SAP Lead to Cash for Cloud Deployment (L2C) has long been recognized as a powerful process for managing the end-to-end sales process, from lead generation to order fulfillment. Now, SAP has unveiled a tailored variant of Lead to cash specifically designed for Consumer Industries: Lead to Cash (Consumer Industries B2C) for Cloud Deployment.

Lead to Cash for Consumer Industries is an all-encompassing process that empowers organizations to optimize their sales operations, provide personalized customer experiences, and gain actionable insights to drive growth. This variant is strategically developed to address the unique challenges and requirements of consumer-focused businesses, offering a range of advanced features and capabilities to address the specific needs of the industry.

One of the key highlights of Lead to Cash for Consumer Industries is its integrated omnichannel capabilities. Companies today understand the importance of providing a consistent and seamless customer experience across various touchpoints, including online, in-store and mobile. With Lead to Cash for Consumer Industries, businesses can effectively manage sales and customer interactions across multiple channels, ensuring a cohesive and personalized experience at every touchpoint.

In the new SAP End-to-End process (Lead to Cash - Consumer Industries B2C), SAP Order Management Foundation (OMF) plays a central role. Some of the business benefits of SAP OMF include increased automation of order processing, improved order accuracy, enhanced visibility into order status, and streamlined order fulfillment. Please follow this link to learn more about SAP Order Management Foundation.

 

Explore the new variant:

Lead to cash (Consumer Industries B2C) for Cloud Deployment

 

Learn More about Lead to Cash and Integration by checking out our latest whitepaper:

Integrate to Innovate

entry.title: SAP Grantor Management – What is new for the S/4HANA 2023 FPS2 release
entry.content:
In my first two blogs (Grantor management in S/4HANA  and Grantor Management - What is new since S/4HANA 2022 release) on SAP Grantor Management in S/4HANA, I covered all the features and functions introduced from the initial 2022 release up to the S/4HANA 2023 FPS1 release. In this post, I will discuss what’s new in the S/4HANA 2023 FPS2 release:

Virtual Data Model for grantor management

With this new virtual data model we provide straightforward and harmonized access to the data model for grantor management

Key benefits:

  1. Access to grantor-management data from embedded as well as external analytical tools for SAP S/4HANA
  2. Tool usage without the need to know the technical environment in which the data persists

 

Real-Time Reporting and Analytics for Grantor Management with Fiori Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and Drill-Downs

With these KPIs we provide an overview of the most important key performance indicators for grantor applications, agreements, and claims

Key benefits:

  1. Enable a unique user experience with a Web-based application and intuitive-to-consume UI
  2. Gain real-time insight into key information about grantor applications, agreements, and claims

For more information, see:

 My Applications Fiori App

My Agreements Fiori App 

My Claims Fiori App 

Business Warehouse data extraction and replication based on the virtual data model for grantor management

This development introduces new Business Warehouse extractors built on Core Data Services (CDS) views using the ABAP programming language for:

  • Grantor programs
  • Grantor applications
  • Grantor agreements
  • Grantor claims

For more information, see

Grantor Application - CDS view for data extraction 

Grantor Agreement - CDS view for data extraction 

Grantor Claim - CDS view for data extraction 

 

Stay tuned for more insights in the next post! Until then, thanks for reading, and feel free to share your thoughts or questions in the comments. See you next time!

Diego Gaudenzi, Grantor Management Product Owner

entry.title: Comprendre les Firefighter IDs dans SAP
entry.content:
Dans le cadre de la gestion des accès d’urgence dans SAP, le processus de FireFighter IDs (ou Emergency Access Management, EAM) joue un rôle crucial en matière de sécurité et de gestion des risques. Ce processus permet aux utilisateurs de disposer d’un accès temporaire à des comptes avec plus d’autorisations, tout en assurant un suivi rigoureux de leurs actions.

Qu’est-ce qu’un Firefighter ID dans SAP ?

Un Firefighter ID est un identifiant qui offre à un utilisateur un accès temporaire à des autorisations et des comptes avec des droits et autorisations supérieures, souvent nécessaires pour répondre à des situations d'urgence ou critiques pour l’entreprise. Cet accès est distinct des permissions quotidiennes de l’utilisateur et permet de séparer les accès sensibles des autorisations régulières. Ce rôle de FireFighter s’accompagne de mécanismes de suivi détaillés pour garantir une traçabilité complète des actions effectuées.

Pourquoi le processus EAM est-il nécessaire ?

Le processus EAM consiste à octroyer des accès temporaires à des utilisateurs spécifiques afin qu’ils puissent accomplir des tâches qui sortent de leur champ d’autorisation habituel. Cela est particulièrement utile en cas de problèmes urgents où une intervention rapide est requise. Chaque action effectuée sous un Firefighter ID est méticuleusement enregistrée pour permettre un audit ultérieur, garantissant ainsi la transparence et la sécurité des opérations.

Défis liés à la mise en œuvre d’un processus EAM

  1. Définir correctement les comptes d’utilisateur et de FireFighter : L’un des principaux défis est de bien définir la distinction entre les comptes d’utilisateur classiques et les FireFighter IDs. Il est essentiel de trouver un équilibre entre les fonctions quotidiennes de l'utilisateur et l'utilisation d’un FireFighter ID, réservé aux situations d’urgence. Une mauvaise gestion de cet équilibre peut entraîner une utilisation abusive de l’ID FireFighter pour des tâches routinières, ce qui surcharge les employés responsables des audits.
  2. Retards dans les audits des journaux : Un autre défi est d’assurer des audits rapides après l'utilisation d'un FireFighter ID. Si les audits ne sont pas réalisés à temps, des risques potentiels comme la fraude ou des vulnérabilités de sécurité peuvent persister sans être détectés. Le manque de documentation sur les raisons d’accès d’urgence complique également l’audit, rendant la tâche des administrateurs plus difficile.
  1. Difficulté à interpréter les journaux EAM : Les journaux EAM dans SAP GRC peuvent être complexes à analyser pour les utilisateurs métiers non techniques. Cela peut conduire à des validations inappropriées, où les actions effectuées sous le FireFighter ID ne sont pas entièrement comprises avant l'approbation des audits.

Méthodologies FireFighter

Il existe deux principales méthodologies pour l’utilisation des FireFighter IDs dans SAP :

  • Méthodologie par utilisateur : L'utilisateur dispose d’un identifiant distinct pour ses tâches quotidiennes et d’un FireFighter ID pour les activités d’urgence.
  • Méthodologie par rôle : L’utilisateur garde le même identifiant pour toutes ses tâches, mais un rôle spécifique de FireFighter lui est attribué en cas d’urgence.

Définir l’accès pour le processus EAM

Deux options sont généralement considérées pour la gestion des accès :

  • Accès pré-approuvé : L’utilisateur dispose d'une autorisation préalable pour accéder au FireFighter ID. Cela permet une réponse rapide, mais présente des risques de sécurité plus élevés.
  • Accès avec approbation supplémentaire : L’utilisateur doit demander une autorisation avant d'utiliser le FireFighter ID. Cette méthode est plus sécurisée mais peut entraîner des retards.

entry.title: How Do You Decide Your SAP S/4HANA Migration Path?
entry.content:
How do you decide your SAP S/4HANA migration path? This is an overwhelming challenge to any CTO organization who is on SAP ECC system.

 

There are a lot of factors hitting enterprises today, such as the adoption of a hybrid cloud architecture, native S/4HANA public cloud solutions and integrating SAP S/4HANA with other tools. The road to migrate an organization’s ERP architecture to SAP S/4HANA starts with understanding the hurdles one need to overcome in this transformation. The choice of the system integrator is equally as important, a partner that will turn your initiative into an executable project.

 

SAP S/4HANA is SAP’s intelligent, integrated ERP system that’s designed to run on SAP’s in-memory HANA database. It can be deployed on premises, in a public or private cloud, or in a hybrid environment. This replaces SAP ECC ERP suite of enterprise applications. ECC (Enterprise Core Components) was designed to run on a third-party database, such as Oracle or SQL Server. S/4HANA comes with SAP's own in-memory HANA database. Application Lifecycle Management periodically requires to upgrade or migrate to the latest of SAP ERP package. The move to S/4HANA provides once in a generation opportunities for customers to operate more efficiently and supports their larger digital journeys.

 

Since SAP is sunsetting SAP ECC 6.0 in 2027 (extended support till 2030), customers have to migrate SAP S/4HANA. Not meeting SAP’s deadlines will limit your ability to execute digital transformation, adapt new innovations including AI, able to use other 3rd party add-on software, leverage better user experience, improved performance, and reduced total cost of ownership. Additionally, since SAP S/4HANA is a more secure and easier to maintain platform. For all customers, small, medium, and large, it’s not a matter of “if” they have to migrate, but “when.”

 

However, it’s not always easy for organizations to move away from a business model that has been highly successful. It can also be challenging to commit to a new mindset, implement new technologies, and obtain the employee buy-in necessary to succeed. Organization change management will play an important role. It is no more an IT driven initiative, it will bring in a new operating model and a culture that brings together the business, the IT drivers, and talent. This intersection of technology, strategy, and talent is precisely where system integrators come in.

 

Migration to SAP S/4HANA comes with few option. If a company decide to implement S/4HANA public cloud SaaS solution, it will be completely greenfield. If a company decides to retain a in-premise or private cloud solution, there are three main approaches to roll out SAP S/4HANA: the greenfield approach, the brownfield approach, or a hybrid of the two.  Depending on a few key factors, such as your company’s use of SAP, the size and state of your data, the technical debt you carry, and your future business needs, the technical approach can be selected to build the business case. Customers use this opportunity to make the most of innovation, overhaul business processes, adopt standard out of box solutions and adopt cloud solutions. If customer have a heavy customization invested in SAP ECC and want to keep your existing data, and go live with a big bang approach, a system conversion is the way. This is also an opportunity to consolidate multiple SAP ECC instances. If customer decide to adapt a combination of the approaches in a phased program, that too is possible. There are enough opportunity for selecting the right approach tailored to suite you.

 

To help SAP customers decide between a Brownfield (system conversion) or Greenfield (new implementation) approach to transitioning to SAP S/4HANA, asking the right questions can help identify the most suitable path or evolve something in-between. These questions below will provide a comprehensive view of the customer’s needs and can guide the decision between Brownfield or Greenfield implementation.

 

 

AreaLeading Queries

1. Current System Evaluation

  1. How customized is your current SAP ERP system? (heavily customized systems may require more effort in a Brownfield approach) Do  you have a need to keep you existing solution enhancements or transaction data history?
  2. How well is your current system meeting business needs? Are there critical gaps or inefficiencies?
  3. Do you have a clear understanding of existing data quality? How much data cleansing would be required?

2. Business Strategy and Objectives

  1. What are your company’s long-term digital transformation goals?
  2. Are there significant business process changes required to align with S/4HANA's capabilities, or do you want to retain most existing processes?
  3. Do you plan to standardize your processes according to SAP best practices, or will you need to maintain a high level of customization?

3. Budget and Timeline

  1. What is your budget for the S/4HANA project? (Greenfield implementations can often be more expensive upfront, while Brownfield may have lower costs but less flexibility.)
  2. What are your timeline constraints? (Greenfield projects might take longer due to redesigning processes, while Brownfield conversions could be faster but require extensive testing.)
  3. Are you looking for a phased migration or a big-bang approach?

4. Risk Tolerance and Change Management

  1. How much disruption to business operations can your organization tolerate during the migration? (Business downtime)
  2. How comfortable is your organization with managing change? Are you prepared to undergo significant organizational and process changes (typically required in Greenfield)?
  3. How much invested your business will be in this transformation? What is your organization’s risk appetite? (Greenfield offers fresh opportunities but comes with the risk of business process re-engineering.)

5. Data Management

  1. How important is it to retain historical data in the new system? Are you planning on a complete or partial data migration?
  2. How mature is your data governance strategy? A Greenfield approach often requires more rigorous data management.
  3. How organized and clean are your master data, analytics data and transactional data? Do you archive your data?

6. Technical Architecture

  1. What is the state of your current system landscape? Is it up to date, or are there legacy systems that may not easily transition in a Brownfield scenario? Do you have a choice where your systems are hosted? Is your organization open for Hyperscale hosted solution?
  2. How scalable and flexible does your future SAP environment need to be? Are you interested in taking advantage of new technologies like SAP Fiori, AI, and IoT?
  3. Are there existing integrations with third-party systems that need to be considered, and how would they impact each approach?

7. Future-Proofing

  1. Do you want to future-proof your business processes for the next 10-15 years by adopting a completely new, SAP-standard model (Greenfield)?
  2. Is your organization sees value and is ready to adapt SAP best practices and take help of 'fit-to-standard' approach for process adaptation? 
  3. How do your business perceive your current system – as an Innovation Blocker or Key Asset?

8. Compliance and Regulatory Requirements

  1. Are there any regulatory, compliance, or industry-specific requirements that would impact the migration approach?
  2. Are you under any external pressure to meet certain standards or deadlines (e.g., tax compliance, environmental regulations)?
  3. Do you have any system decommissioning requirement like retaining legacy data or older systems for certain number of years?

9. End User and IT Support Impact

  1. What impact will the chosen approach have on end users, and how will training and adoption be managed?
  2. How dependent are your business processes on the current system’s user interfaces and workflows?
  3. What is the current state of your IT support structure? Does your IT team have the skills to support a Greenfield project, or are they more comfortable maintaining and testing the existing system in a Brownfield approach?

 

When you indulge in the dialog on these areas, you will bring out a general picture with data points to take the enterprise roadmap discussion ahead. You will capture data points and have constructive follow-on discussions to help create the business case. Situational and organizational knowledge plays a vital role. It can be difficult to decide immediately which SAP S/4HANA implementation strategy is right for you, however, the first step to define the business need and data to start building a business case will be achieved.

entry.title: Rethinking AI’s Role in SAP: End of Bureaucracy = a Path to True Agility and Innovation
entry.content:
AI only for SAP Consultants?

In the world of SAP IT, many believe that AI will make developers and functional teams more productive by automating tasks and helping with decision-making. This, in theory, should make work faster and everyone happier. However, there's a major concern—many consultants and workers are worried that AI will take away their jobs. This fear causes resistance to AI and slows down further automation efforts.

But focusing only on how AI can help developers and functional teams might be too narrow. AI could be even more helpful if it were used to simplify the bureaucratic processes that slow down innovation in large organizations. Over the years, many SAP projects have tried to use agile methods, which focus on working quickly and adapting to changes. Unfortunately, they often fail because of outdated processes and too much bureaucracy, which make it hard to truly embrace agile.

 

So, instead of thinking about AI as just a tool to improve developer productivity, why not consider how it could reduce bureaucracy and make SAP projects more agile? In large organizations with thousands of employees, complex approval processes and heavy paperwork slow down progress. This makes it hard to move quickly or innovate.

AI to enable SAP innovation by reducing management operations 

AI could be used to simplify and automate project management tasks like resource allocation, project timelines, and risk management. It could help streamline decision-making and approval processes, allowing project managers to focus on bigger-picture strategy instead of getting bogged down in administrative work. If AI can take over these time-consuming tasks, project teams could collaborate more effectively and work faster.

In a large SAP environment, AI could remove the red tape that holds back agile workflows. This would allow companies to move towards true innovation, without the fear of job losses. In fact, AI could create new opportunities for employees to focus on strategic work, rather than repetitive tasks, thus increasing their value in the organization.

Conclusion - start with eliminating the bureaucratic barriers

In conclusion, AI’s true potential in SAP isn’t just about making developers and functional teams more productive. It’s about breaking down the bureaucratic barriers that prevent organizations from becoming agile and innovative. By applying AI to these outdated processes, companies can speed up project delivery, foster creativity, and unlock the full benefits of agile methodologies.

Want more?

Follow https://www.linkedin.com/in/michalkrawczyk/ on linked for a new episode of my webcast where I will discuss this topic in detail.

entry.title: Enhancing Compliance with BR-KSA-08: A Localization Solution for Multi-Branch Organizations
entry.content:
In response to Saudi Arabia’s evolving e-invoicing regulations, specifically the validation rule BR-KSA-08, a new localization solution has been developed to streamline compliance for organizations operating multiple branches under a single sales organization. This rule mandates that sellers must include the commercial registration number (CRN) of the specific branch issuing the tax invoice in the edocument submitted to ZATCA, the tax authority.

In this blog post, we will delve into the newly released configuration options for SAP S/4HANA that address this requirement, ensuring your organization's invoicing processes align seamlessly with the regulatory standards.

The Challenge: Multi-Branch CRN Management

For organizations operating in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (KSA), adhering to the validation rule BR-KSA-08 presents a unique challenge, particularly for those with multiple branches under one sales organization. Each branch might have a distinct commercial registration number (CRN) which must be accurately reflected in tax invoices. Failing to comply can lead to regulatory issues and potential fines, making it crucial to correctly manage and populate the appropriate CRNs.

The Solution: Tailored Configuration for Compliance

To address this challenge, a robust solution has been developed for both on-premise (OP) and cloud-based SAP S/4HANA customers. This solution leverages specialized configuration paths to ensure that the correct CRN is populated at the appropriate organizational level, thereby simplifying compliance with BR-KSA-08.

CRN Management at Sales Office Level:

  • Multiple Branches, Multiple CRNs:
    • When multiple branches operate under a single sales organization, and these branches are defined as sales offices, each having a unique CRN, this CRN can now be maintained at the sales office level.
  • Automated CRN Reference:
    • During the billing process, the system will reference the CRN configured at the sales office level.
    • In the absence of a specific CRN for a sales office, the system will default to using the CRN maintained at the company code level in the additional parameter CREGNO.
  • Applicability:
    • This configuration is only applicable for SD billing documents based edocuments.

For SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Customers:

  1. Configuration Path Creation:
    • Navigate to the MENA --> General Settings --> Maintain Additional Fiscal Data for Sales Office customizing activity.
    • You can also access the configuration directly by searching for the GLO_TVBURV view using the transaction code SM30.

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Customers:

  1. New SSCUI Introduction:
    • A new Self-Service Configuration UI (SSCUI) - 106929 has been introduced for cloud customers, facilitating the configuration of additional fiscal data at the sales office level.
  2. User Convenience:
    • The SSCUI streamlines the process, allowing cloud customers to effortlessly input and manage the unique CRNs for each branch. Selection path: in the localization sub application area under the Application Area: Finance 
    • This ensures that the appropriate CRN is automatically populated during invoice generation, maintaining compliance with minimal manual intervention.

Important Considerations:

  • Sales Office at Line Item Level:
    • Sales office is at the line item level in the billing document and it is assumed that all line items of the billing document have sales office and that they are the same. Having different sales offices in the billing document or having the same sales office for a few and blank for others is not an allowed scenario as per the tax authority. This is because billing of the goods/services sold should be from one branch per billing document. Allowed scenarios are all line items should have the same sales office, or the sales office in all line items should be blank. This is a manual practice that the users need to ensure.
  • CRN Determination:
    • Under the above assumption, the solution reads only the first line item and checks if a CRN is maintained in this new configuration for that sales office. If not maintained, then the CRN maintained in the company code additional parameter (CREGNO) is read to be populated in the XML.

Conclusion

Compliance with Saudi Arabia's BR-KSA-08 validation rule is critical for organizations with multiple branches, and the newly released localization solution for SAP S/4HANA on-premise and cloud platforms offers a seamless way to meet this requirement. By enabling the accurate and automated population of commercial registration numbers, this solution not only simplifies compliance but also enhances operational efficiency.

Stay ahead of regulatory changes and streamline your tax invoicing processes by leveraging these new configurations and tools. For detailed guidance and implementation support, reach out to your SAP consultant or local SAP support center.

entry.title: Revolutionized What's New Viewer Experience in SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
We have made an exciting new change in our What’s New Viewer for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. The viewer now lets you compare new features and changes between SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, and check which are available in both.

This is made possible because both products share a single What’s New Viewer. Your default scope selection will depend on where you access the What’s New Viewer from; either the SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition product pages on SAP Help Portal.

You can change the scope by using the options in the “Availability” filter. By selecting SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition you will view the features and changes that are available for both products. Selecting either SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition will filter to features and changes only available for that individual product.

We hope this update will give a broader understanding to both products, and support your daily What’s New Viewer experience.

entry.title: Breathing New Life into SAP Brownfield+: 5 Challenges and 5 Steps with BTP, UX, and Innovation Trife
entry.content:
Background

Brownfield projects+, while offering a tempting shortcut to expansion, come with their own set of hurdles. Despite the acknowledged limitations of brownfield+ upgrades compared to greenfield projects, many customers are drawn to this approach due to the significant risks and costs associated with greenfield implementations.

The Challenge and The Step

1. Integrated Legacy Systems: The Weight of the Past

Outdated integrated systems is a common obstacle in brownfield projects. Integrating new systems with legacy infrastructure can lead to compatibility issues, data silos, and security vulnerabilities and may also need clients to rewire the integrations to the new system.

Consolidation of Integrations: . We should begin by ranking each integration based on its business criticality and the potential positive business impact of modernization. We still need to rewire all the old integrations to the new SAP Brownfield + systems and test them to ensure that data is flowing through correctly and processes are working as expected. This may be right time for us to assess whether rewiring or upgrading middle-ware or redesigning interfaces using new BTP technologies is the right thing to do based on risk vs reward vs cost.

 

Conquer Integration Chaos

1. Minimize Integration Debt: Identify and eliminate unnecessary integrations, focusing on those that deliver clear business value.

2. Prioritize Integration Journeys: Define and prioritize key integration journeys that directly impact business efficiencies and outcomes.

3. Establish Integration Standards:Implement clear standards and best practices for future integrations to ensure consistency, maintainability, and scalability.

2. Data Inconsistencies: Unearthing Hidden Treasures

Brownfield+ projects often inherit messy data landscapes. Inconsistent formats, duplicate entries, and poor data quality hinder decision-making and operational efficiency.

Unearthing Hidden Treasures : Now is the ideal time to uncover valuable data assets and establish robust data governance. We should identify business processes where analytic insights and improving poor data quality can yield significant business benefits. Then, we can define and implement appropriate data policies and governance mechanisms to ensure data accuracy, consistency, and reliability in these areas.

 

3 data gold mine tips, link process and data and analytics KPI's together !
  1. Fill Data Holes : Leverage process mining tools to identify data holes that are causing process inefficiencies
  2. Magnify : Assess and rank both business processes and data objects based on their potential business value and impact on desired outcomes.
  3. Build Process and Data Telescope : Prioritize process analytic insights and attributes that helps to see your business and processes in different dimensions and re-think analytic journey and get data quality right.

3. Spice up the Brownfield+ User Adoption: Use the UX as a cornerstone

Don't let UX become an afterthought in brownfield+ projects, this can be crucial factor to sell your S4 HANA brownfield + business case to business! Prioritize customer and business user experience by identifying key customer and business persona journeys that would benefit from a refresh. Leverage the power of generative AI, Web4, and 5G technologies to seamlessly embed modern and engaging UX elements into these end to end journeys.

 

Don't think SAP, think journeys end to end to identify areas of highest business impact!
  1. Engage UX and Business Experts : Conduct a poll to identify customer and business journeys that can be improved
  2. Heart the high impact UX journeys : Map user journeys to business KPI's and get businesses to heart/like the UX journeys that will be be very useful to business
  3. Magic Business UX Value Formulae : Use Gen AI to map UX journeys to value case and develop UX Journeys cost vs value case

4. Grease the Wheels and Prune the Code

While achieving a pristine core codebase might seem like a distant dream in brownfield+ projects with extensive legacy code, remember that even seemingly insurmountable challenges can be overcome with bold vision and determined action. If Elon Musk can revolutionize the automotive and aerospace industries, surely we can find innovative ways to streamline SAP codebase. Let's not be daunted by the scale of the task, but instead, approach it with the same spirit of innovation and ambition that drives groundbreaking achievements.

 

May not be fully clean but easier to scale in future
  1. Trimming the Fat : Time for some code spring cleaning! Let's hunt down those unused custom objects and code, then hit delete and free up some space in our system
  2. Polish Your Custom Crown Jewels : Let's sharpen our competitive edge! We'll leverage cutting-edge BTP,AI and cloud technologies to revamp and supercharge the custom objects that set us apart in the market.
  3. No More Code Clutter: Time for a Fresh Future: Let's create a sunset plan for currently, moderately used, outdated code. We'll map out how to redesign or retire this existing code, set clear expiration dates, and establish guidelines to ensure all new custom code is future-proof and aligned with our long-term goals.

5. Create a Landscape That Bends, Not Breaks

ERP and legacy modernization projects often become overly complex and costly due to a perfect storm of challenges: inadequate documentation, lack of automated testing for continuous improvement, a poor understanding of existing data and processes, and insufficient availability of crucial business resources. Brownfield+ projects offer a prime opportunity to **bleep** these recurring project nightmares in the bud.

 

DevOps: The Conductor of Operational Symphony
  1. Instant Process Mapping where Gen AI Meets Signavio : Leverage new Gen AI and Signavio tools to augment your process flows and generate documentations for critical end to end process scenarios.
  2. Shift Gears for Continuous Automated Testing : Don't let those end-to-end brownfield test scripts gather dust! Automate them, document them, and turn them into a valuable asset for future testing and maintenance of your brownfield project.
  3. The Change Express for delivering value non-stop : Reuse change management training and enable continuous release mechanisms where possible to deploy applications quicker where possible using new GIT frameworks. Let's inject some fun into change management! Imagine a Gen AI bot as your guide, helping you create engaging podcasts, interactive diagrams, and other creative content to communicate process changes in a fun and memorable way.

By embracing a strategic blend of BTP, UX design, and a forward-thinking mindset, we can transform brownfield projects into catalysts for innovation and growth.

Now go forth and conquer those brownfield challenges! We have the power to transform brownfield limitations into strategic advantages.

Don't shy away from bold goals. Even seemingly daunting legacy systems can be modernized and optimized for a brighter future.
Prioritize user experience. A seamless and intuitive UX can make all the difference in driving user adoption and maximizing the value of your brownfield investment.
Embrace automation and continuous improvement. From code optimization to testing and deployment, automation is key to unlocking efficiency and agility.
Make it fun! Change management doesn't have to be dry and tedious. Inject creativity and engagement into the process to inspire your team and stakeholders.
